text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy using a standard four-port technique. The gallbladder is grasped and retracted cranially, while Hartmann's pouch is retracted upwards and to the right, facilitating the dissection of Calot's triangle.
The surgeon dissects the Calot's triangle, carefully removing fat from the area while ensuring hemostasis. The cystic duct is identified and separated from the cystic artery within the triangle.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector and hook, along with careful diathermy, to dissect further strands of areolar tissue, providing a clear view of the cystic duct, cystic artery, and the base of the gallbladder. The critical view of safety is achieved.

A clip is applied to the lower end of Hartman's pouch, followed by a small incision to introduce the cholangiogram catheter. The catheter is flushed with saline and then with contrast, revealing normal anatomy without common bile duct stones.
The surgeon clips and cuts the cystic duct and cystic artery, completing the devascularization of the gallbladder during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon continues the cranial dissection, separating the gallbladder from the liver bed. Precision is emphasized to identify any abnormal ducts, such as the ducts of Luschka, that may emerge from the liver bed.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the thin-walled gallbladder at the upper end of the gallbladder fossa, taking precautions to avoid perforation that could lead to bile leakage or strain on the liver, which might cause hemorrhage.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder and applies appropriate tension to expose the important plane, using a hook to perform dissection while ensuring excellent hemostasis during the initial phase.

The surgeon addresses areas requiring hemostasis near the thin liver edge. A BERT bag is inserted into the abdominal cavity, and the gallbladder is placed inside it to minimize wound infection risk during extraction. The gallbladder is extracted through the umbilical port.
The surgeon performs a washout of the abdominal cavity, using clear fluid to rinse the area. There is no visible evidence of bile or blood in the fluid.
The camera is repositioned to the epigastric port, and a 10mm gallbladder grasper is inserted through the umbilical port. The gallbladder is then extracted using the grasper.
